title: Regular Expression Matching
problem_statement: Implement regex matching with support for "." and "*" where "." matches any character and "*" matches zero or more of the preceding element.

examples.explanation: c* can match empty, then a* matches "aa" and b matches b.

intuition: DP on positions with states handling * as zero or multiple matches.

approaches.name: Recursive Brute Force
approaches.description: Directly recurse on the string and pattern. At each step, check if the first characters match. If the next pattern character is '*', branch into two choices: skip the '*' pair entirely, or consume one character from s if it matches and stay at the same pattern position.
approaches.why_not: Exponential time due to overlapping subproblems being recomputed. Will TLE on inputs near the upper constraint bounds.
interview_commentary: Before coding, I just want to make sure I understand the problem fully.
When we see 'x*' in the pattern, x* can match zero or more x's — so 'c*' can match empty string, 'c', 'cc', etc.
The recursion mirrors exactly how we'd reason through it by hand — at each step ask: does this position match, and move forward.
The problem is that we'll hit the same (i, j) pair many times — that's the classic signal to memoize or use DP.

approaches.name: Memoized Recursion (Top-Down DP)
approaches.description: Same recursion as brute force, but cache results in a HashMap keyed by (i, j) — the current indices into s and p. This eliminates repeated computation of identical subproblems.
approaches.space: O(m * n) for the memo table
approaches.why_not: Still has recursion overhead and the key construction cost per call. Bottom-up DP is cleaner and avoids stack depth concerns for large inputs.
interview_commentary: Noticing the overlapping subproblems, let me add memoization. I'll key the cache on the pair (i, j) — current position in s and current position in p.
This brings us down to O(m*n) time because each unique (i, j) is computed exactly once.
The logic is identical to brute force — only difference is we check the cache before recursing and store before returning.
This is good enough to pass, but let me think if we can do better with bottom-up DP to avoid recursion stack and string key overhead.

approaches.name: Bottom-Up Dynamic Programming
approaches.description: Build a 2D dp table where dp[i][j] = true if s[i:] matches p[j:]. Fill the table from the end backwards. The base case dp[m][n] = true (both exhausted). Patterns ending in 'x*' sequences can also match an empty string. For each cell, apply the same two-branch logic as the recursive solution but iteratively.
approaches.why_not: This is the optimal solution. Space can theoretically be optimized to O(n) with rolling arrays, but that's rarely asked in interviews.
interview_commentary: Let me define dp[i][j] as: 'does s[i:] match p[j:]?' — suffix-based DP is more natural here because '*' always refers to the preceding pattern character.

Base cases first: dp[m][n] = true (both exhausted — empty matches empty). Then I walk the last row backwards in steps of 2: any trailing 'x*' sequence can match an empty string by using zero occurrences.

For the '*' branch I have exactly two choices mirroring the recursive solution: skip the x* pair entirely (Option A: dp[i][j+2]), or consume one char from s and stay at j to allow more matches (Option B: firstMatch && dp[i+1][j]).
For the plain branch it's a direct one-for-one: current chars must match and both pointers advance to dp[i+1][j+1].
I loop i from m-1 down to 0 because dp[i][j] depends on dp[i+1][...] — the row below must already be filled before we use it.

main() runs a suite of 14 hand-crafted tests — including the provided example, chained 'x*' against empty string, dot-star combos, and no-match cases — and prints PASS/FAIL for each. It also accepts two command-line args for quick ad-hoc testing.
Final answer sits at dp[0][0]: does all of s match all of p? Time O(m*n), Space O(m*n).

interview_qa.q: Walk me through why you chose to fill the DP table bottom-up instead of top-down.
interview_qa.a: Bottom-up avoids recursion stack overhead and function call costs per state. Since every cell dp[i][j] depends only on dp[i][j+2] and dp[i+1][j], filling from bottom-right to top-left gives us a clean, predictable traversal with no stack depth risk. Top-down with memoization is equally correct and O(m*n) — I'd use it in a pinch if I couldn't recall the iteration order — but bottom-up is cleaner to reason about in an interview.

interview_qa.q: Can you reduce the space from O(m*n) to O(n)?
What if '?' was also a wildcard that matches exactly one character — how would your solution change?
interview_qa.a:
Almost nothing changes. In the firstMatch check, I already handle '.' as 'any single character'. I'd just add '|| p.charAt(j) == '?'' to the same condition:

boolean firstMatch = (p.charAt(j) == s.charAt(i)
                   || p.charAt(j) == '.'
                   || p.charAt(j) == '?');

The rest of the DP logic — including how '*' works — stays identical because '?' doesn't change the two-pointer movement rules, only the single-character matching condition.

interview_qa.q: How does your solution handle the edge case where the pattern is just ".*"?
interview_qa.a: Let's trace s='abc', p='.*'. At i=0, j=0: p[0]='.' and p[1]='*' so we hit the '*' branch. dp[0][0] = dp[0][2] OR (firstMatch && dp[1][0]). dp[0][2] checks if s[0:]='abc' matches p[2:]='' — false. But firstMatch is true ('.' matches 'a'), so we check dp[1][0]: does s[1:]='bc' match p[0:]='.*'? Same logic recurses all the way to dp[3][0]: does s[3:]='' match '.*'? That's the base case row where dp[m][0] was initialized to true (zero occurrences of '.'). So the whole chain resolves to true.

interview_qa.q: Could there be a pattern that causes your loop bounds to go out of range?
interview_qa.a: I guard against this in two places. First, I only enter the i loop from m-1 down to 0 (not m), so s.charAt(i) is always a valid access — no out-of-bounds on s. Second, the '*' lookahead checks 'j + 1 < n' before touching p.charAt(j+1). The base-case initialization loop also steps j by 2 and breaks early on a non-'*' pair, so it never reads past the pattern. Together these three guards make every array access provably in-range.
